narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 75.8% chance that you would not pass, based on patterns from thousands of past students. This high predicted risk of failure triggered the rejection to help the university maintain strong bar passage rates for its graduates. The model's decision hinged most strongly on your academic decile in your third year of school, which ranked quite low and stood out as the biggest factor pushing the prediction toward failure—far below the levels typical for students who went on to pass the bar. Your first-year decile also played a significant role in the same direction, signaling early struggles that echoed challenges seen in students who later failed the exam. Interestingly, your LSAT score provided the strongest counterbalance, pulling the prediction somewhat toward success since it showed solid potential compared to many who passed. While your undergraduate GPA and middle-family-income background were considered, along with your choice to study full-time like most successful students, they didn't outweigh the concerns from your school deciles. Compared to admitted students, who typically ranked in the top half of their classes (around the 6th decile for both first and third years) and had higher average LSATs near 37 and GPAs around 3.24, your profile aligned more closely with those who faced bar exam hurdles. This isn't a judgment on your effort or potential—many students with similar starts have improved dramatically—but the model prioritizes these proven indicators of bar success. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost that positive influence or strengthening your class rankings through targeted academic support before reapplying. You might also explore programs with bar prep resources tailored for full-time students from middle-income backgrounds. Gathering letters from professors who can highlight your growth could help contextualize your record. With focused steps like these, you can reshape your profile to better match successful applicants next time. Keep pushing—you've already shown capability on the LSAT that the model noticed.
